frameworks.name: evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: neural integration of sensory evidence toward a decision distributed across brain regions

frameworks.name: population subspace dynamics
frameworks.one_line: shared low-dimensional patterns encode evidence and movement preparation distinct from execution

frameworks.name: movement-preparatory activity
frameworks.one_line: preparatory subspace driven by integrator neurons that collapse at movement onset

frameworks.name: intrinsic regional timescales
frameworks.one_line: integration timescales found independent of intrinsic dynamics, depending instead on task experience

core_question: How are sensation, evidence integration, motor planning and execution orchestrated across brain regions and neural dimensions during learned perceptual decisions?
key_assumptions: decision-making engages many brain regions, not a single locus
neural population activity can be decomposed into task-relevant subspaces
learning reshapes how regions participate in evidence accumulation
manipulations: ambiguous visual input reported by mice via change detection
brain-wide recording across cortical, thalamic, basal ganglia, midbrain and cerebellar areas
key_findings: evidence integration emerges in sparse populations across most brain areas after learning
visual responses shift from transient sensory activations to sustained frontal-motor, thalamic, basal ganglia, midbrain and cerebellar representations
shared population patterns encode evidence and movement preparation, distinct from execution dynamics
premotor evidence-integration timescales are independent of intrinsic regional dynamics
interpretations: learning aligns evidence accumulation with action preparation across dozens of brain regions
preparatory subspace collapse at movement onset resets the integration process
